articles.title: 90-100 KM wind, heavy rain, thunderstorms and...: Delhi, Noida, Gurugram, Uttar Pradesh, Rajasthan, Punjab, Bihar Jharkhand to..., IMD issues alert for 13 states; check full IMD forecast - news24online.com
articles.full_text:
90-100 KM wind, heavy rain, thunderstorms and…: Delhi, Noida, Gurugram, Uttar Pradesh, Rajasthan, Punjab, Bihar Jharkhand to…, IMD issues alert for 13 states; check full IMD forecast
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued an alert for thunderstorms and heavy rainfall in as many as 13 states, which is expected to bring some relief from the scorching heatwave conditions prevailing in northern India, including in Delhi-NCR and parts of Uttar Pradesh.
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued an alert for thunderstorms and heavy rainfall in as many as 13 states, which is expected to bring some relief from the scorching heatwave conditions prevailing in northern India, including in Delhi-NCR and parts of Uttar Pradesh.
IMD issues storm alert for 13 states
As per the IMD weather warning, weather is expected to change is several parts of the country over the next 72 hours, with the agency issuing alerts for as many as 13 states, where torrential rain, accompanied by dust storms, lightning and powerful gusty winds with speeds up to 100 kmph are expected to occur. According to meteorologists, a Western Disturbance and a Cyclonic Circulation are simultaneously becoming active, and effects will be felt from the mountains to the plains and coastal states.
Heatwave alert for Delhi-NCR
The IMD has issued a severe heatwave alert for Delhi and its adjoining NCR districts including Noida, Ghaziabad, Gurugram and Faridabad from May 21-26, with temperatures in the national capital expected to hit 47 degrees Celsius. Dust storms and light showers are possible as the weather begins to turn from May 22 onwards, offering relief from the scorching heat, as per the IMD
IMD issues yellow alert for UP, heatwave to persist
In Uttar Pradesh, the IMD has forecast heatwave conditions to persist over the next few days, and a yellow alert has been issued for several districts, including Kanpur, Prayagraj, Jhansi, Varanasi, Noida, and Agra. Weather is expected to change in parts of western UP after May 23, with rains, thunderstorms, and strong winds likely to lash the region.
Heavy rains, thunderstorms to lash Bihar, Jharkhand
The IMD has issued rain alerts for Patna, Bhagalpur, Darbhanga, Purnia, and Muzaffarpur districts in Bihar, accompanied by strong winds. Lightning and hailstorms are forecast in several districts, while dust storms are possible in areas bordering Nepal, though hot winds will continue in Gaya and southern Bihar. Similar weather conditions are expected to prevail in neighbouring Jharkhand, with powerful winds with speeds up to 70 kmph likely in Ranchi, Jamshedpur, Bokaro, and Dhanbad.
No heatwave relief in Rajasthan, MP
As per the IMD weather alert, heatwave conditions are likely to persist in parts of western Rajasthan, with little relief expected at night. However, dust storms and light rain are expected in Ganganagar and Hanumangarh starting the night of May 22. Madhya Pradesh also continues to reel under scorching heat with hot winds blowing in Bhopal, Gwalior, Rewa, and Chhatarpur. IMD has warned that the heatwave will continue for the next few days. Cloudy skies are expected in some areas, but rain forecasts are weak.
100 kmph winds to hit northeast
According to the IMD forecast, powerful winds with speeds up to 100 kmph are expected to lash parts of northeastern India with heavy rains forecast in Assam, Meghalaya, Arunachal Pradesh, and Sikkim. The agency has cautioned about rains triggering rising water levels in rivers and landslides.

articles.title: Jharkhand Swelters Under Severe Heatwave as Jamshedpur Touches 43°C - Udit Vani
articles.full_text:
UditVani, Jamshedpur: Jharkhand is reeling under intense summer heat, with temperatures soaring across several districts and humidity adding to public discomfort.
Amid the blistering conditions, the India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued a statewide alert for thunderstorms, lightning and gusty winds over the next four days.
Jamshedpur remained one of the hottest places in the state on Wednesday, with the mercury touching 43 degrees Celsius, triggering heatwave-like conditions and forcing residents to stay indoors during peak afternoon hours.
Daltonganj recorded the highest maximum temperature at around 45°C, while Chaibasa touched 43°C.
Ranchi recorded a relatively lower maximum of 39°C, with Namkum registering the state’s lowest minimum temperature at 22.2°C.
While most parts of the state remained dry, scattered rainfall was reported from a few pockets.
“The heat has become unbearable over the last few days. Even stepping out for basic errands in the afternoon feels exhausting. We are avoiding outdoor activities as much as possible,” said Ananya Sinha, a resident of Sonari.
In Bistupur, residents echoed similar concerns. “The roads are nearly empty in the afternoon, and even evenings are unusually warm. We are constantly hydrating and staying indoors whenever possible,” said Rajesh Agarwal, a local resident of Bistupur.
According to the IMD forecast, thunderstorms accompanied by lightning and gusty winds (40–50 kmph) are likely at isolated places across most parts of Jharkhand from May 21 to May 24, excluding portions of the north-western belt.
On May 22, stronger gusts of 50–60 kmph are expected over the north-eastern and adjoining central districts, including Dhanbad and Bokaro, while isolated heatwave conditions may persist in the north-western parts of the state, excluding Latehar.
Similar weather conditions are expected to continue on May 23 and 24, with intensified wind activity forecast for Dhanbad, Bokaro, Giridih, Koderma, Hazaribagh and Ramgarh.
The Meteorological Department has advised residents to avoid exposure to direct sunlight during afternoon hours, stay hydrated, and remain indoors during thunderstorm activity to minimise health and safety risks amid the dual threat of extreme heat and unstable weather.
